title: Renting a boat in Montenegro and the Bay of Kotor
tags: boat rental, yacht charter, Montenegro, travel, Bay of Kotor
topic: Renting a boat in Montenegro and the Bay of Kotor
html:
Montenegro packs an astonishing amount of coastline into a small country, and the best way to see it is from the water. The Bay of Kotor, often called Europe's southernmost fjord, winds inland past medieval towns, steep green mountains and tiny island churches that you simply cannot reach properly by car. Spend a day out there and you understand why so many visitors decide to rent a boat rather than queue for the same crowded viewpoints as everyone else. This post is a practical guide to getting on the water in Montenegro: where to go, what it costs, and how to keep the day relaxed rather than stressful.

Why the Bay of Kotor rewards a boat

The bay is shaped like a butterfly, with two large basins joined by a narrow strait called Verige. Driving the coast road means hairpins, tunnels and limited parking, but a boat cuts straight across the calm inner water. You can drift up to Our Lady of the Rocks, the artificial islet off Perast, then nose along the shoreline below Kotor's old fortifications. Because the bay is sheltered, the water stays flat for most of the day, which makes it forgiving for families and first timers. If you have never skippered before, this is one of the gentler places in the Mediterranean to start.

Bareboat or skippered: which suits you

You have two honest options. A bareboat hire puts you in charge, which is liberating if you hold a recognised licence and feel confident reading wind and traffic. A skippered boat rental hands the wheel to a local who already knows the moorings, the quiet swimming coves and the spots where the afternoon breeze picks up. For a single relaxed day, plenty of travellers prefer a skipper so they can swim, eat and take photos without watching the depth gauge. Think about it like this:

  • Bareboat suits licensed, experienced boaters who want full freedom and a lower headline price.
  • Skippered suits anyone short on time, nervous about local rules, or simply wanting a proper holiday rather than a watch-keeping shift.

What it costs and what to budget for

Prices swing with the season, the size of the vessel and whether fuel is included, so always read the listing carefully before you book. As a rough guide, a small open motorboat for a half day sits at the affordable end, while a crewed yacht for a full day with lunch is a genuine treat. When you compare options and browse boats across different operators, watch for the extras that quietly add up. Typical costs beyond the base rate include:

  1. Fuel, sometimes billed by consumption rather than included.
  2. A refundable security deposit on bareboat hires.
  3. Skipper and crew fees if not already bundled.
  4. Marina or mooring charges at busier towns.
A good rule of thumb: confirm in writing exactly what is and is not included before any money changes hands, then there are no awkward surprises at the dock.

A sample day on the water

Picture a morning departure from Kotor or Tivat. You motor gently across to Perast, hop ashore at Our Lady of the Rocks, then continue to a quiet bay for a long swim while the sun is still soft. Lunch is grilled fish at a waterside konoba, after which you cruise the shaded northern shore back toward base, stopping wherever the water looks too inviting to pass. If you fancy going further afield, some operators run trips out past the bay mouth toward the open Adriatic and the Blue Cave near Herceg Novi, though that stretch is more exposed and better left to a skipper. Whatever route you choose, it is easy to find a vessel that matches both your group size and your appetite for adventure.

Practical tips before you book

A few small habits make the day far smoother. Book ahead in July and August, when demand peaks and the best vessels go early. Check the weather, especially the afternoon wind that can ruffle the open water beyond the bay. Bring sun protection, soft soled shoes and cash for small harbours that do not take cards. Confirm the meeting point precisely, since marinas in Kotor, Tivat and Budva each have several docks. And read recent reviews so you know the boat and the operator are well kept.

Listing your own boat in Montenegro

If you live on the coast and own a vessel that sits idle for much of the week, the charter season is a real opportunity. Demand from visitors far outstrips the supply of well maintained, properly insured boats, particularly skippered ones with local knowledge. You can list your boat on a worldwide marketplace, set your own availability and reach travellers who are searching specifically for the Bay of Kotor. It is a straightforward way to turn quiet weekdays into income while keeping full control of your calendar.

Montenegro is compact, beautiful and surprisingly easy to explore by sea once you know the basics. Decide between bareboat freedom and a skippered escape, budget honestly for the extras, and pick a calm morning for the bay. Do that, and a day on the water here will likely be the part of the trip you remember most clearly long after you have gone home.
